perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the law of England and shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the law of England.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that subjects the agreement to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England could be problematic. While the governing law is specified, not specifying the jurisdiction could lead to disputes about where legal proceedings should occur.  This becomes particularly relevant if one of the parties is not based in England and expected to follow English law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Hague Convention on Choice of Court Agreements
perturbation.law_explanation: The Hague Convention on Choice of Court Agreements aims to establish international standards for the recognition and enforcement of choice of court agreements in civil or commercial matters. By omitting the jurisdiction clause, the contract becomes ambiguous and could potentially conflict with Article 3, which requires such agreements to be exclusive unless otherwise specified. This could lead to jurisdictional disputes and undermine the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Article III | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article III Primary tabs Section 1. The judicial power of the United States, shall be vested in one Supreme Court, and in such inferior courts as the Congress may from time to time ordain and establish. The judges, both of the supreme and inferior courts, shall hold their offices during good behaviour, and shall, at stated times, receive for their services, a compensation, which shall not be diminished during their continuance in office. Section 2. The judicial power shall extend to all cases, in law and equity, arising under this Constitution, the laws of the United States, and treaties made, or which shall be made, under their authority;--to all cases affecting ambassadors, other public ministers and consuls;--to all cases of admiralty and maritime jurisdiction;--to controversies to which the United States shall be a party;--to controversies between two or more states;-- between a state and citizens of another state ;--between citizens of different states;--between citizens of the same state claiming lands under grants of different states, and between a state, or the citizens thereof, and foreign states, citizens or subjects. In all cases affecting ambassadors, other public ministers and consuls, and those in which a state shall be party, the Supreme Court shall have original jurisdiction. In all the other cases before mentioned, the Supreme Court shall have appellate jurisdiction, both as to law and fact, with such exceptions, and under such regulations as the Congress shall make. The trial of all crimes, except in cases of impeachment, shall be by jury; and such trial shall be held in the state where the said crimes shall have been committed; but when not committed within any state, the trial shall be at such place or places as the Congress may by law have directed. Section 3. Treason against the United States, shall consist only in levying war against them, or in adhering to their enemies, giving them aid and comfort. No person shall be convicted of treason unless on the testimony of two witnesses to the same overt act, or on confession in open court. The Congress shall have power to declare the punishment of treason, but no attainder of treason shall work corruption of blood, or forfeiture except during the life of the person attainted. ‹ Article II up Article IV › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ee also defines treason . Section 1 of Article Three vests the judicial power of the United States in "one supreme Court", as well as "inferior courts" established by Congress. Section 1 authorizes the creation of inferior courts, but does not require it; the first inferior federal courts were established shortly after the ratification of the Constitution with the Judiciary Act of 1789 . Section 1 also establishes that federal judges do not face term limits, and that an individual judge's salary may not be decreased. Article Three does not set the size of the Supreme Court or establish specific positions on the court, but Article One establishes the position of chief justice . Along with the Vesting Clauses of Article One and Article Two , Article Three's Vesting Clause establishes the separation of powers among the three branches of government. Section 2 of Article Three delineates federal judicial power. The Case or Controversy Clause restricts the judiciary's power to actual cases and controversies, meaning that federal judicial power does not extend to cases which are hypothetical, or which are proscribed due to standing , mootness , or ripeness issues. Section 2 states that the federal judiciary's power extends to cases arising under the Constitution, federal laws, federal treaties, controversies involving multiple states or foreign powers, and other enumerated areas. Section 2 gives the Supreme Court original jurisdiction when ambassadors, public officials, or the states are a party in the case, leaving the Supreme Court with appellate jurisdiction in all other areas to which the federal judiciary's jurisdiction extends. Section 2 also gives Congress the power to strip the Supreme Court of appellate jurisdiction, and establishes that all federal crimes must be tried before a jury . Section 2 does not expressly grant the federal judiciary the power of judicial review , but the courts have exercised this power since the 1803 case of Marbury v. Madison . Section 3 of Article Three defines treason and empowers Congress to punish treason. Section 3 requires that at least two witnesses testify to the treasonous act, or that the individual accused of treason confess in open court. It also limits the ways in which Congress can punish those convicted of treason. Background [ edit ] Unlike the Articles of Confederation , the US Constitution separated the legislative, executive and judicial powers. Article III separates and places the judicial power in the judiciary. This idea is most often attributed to Montesquieu . Although not the progenitor, Montesquieu's writing on the separation of power in The Spirit of Laws was immensely influential on the U.S. Constitution. [ 1 ] Section 1: Federal courts [ edit ] Section 1 is one of the three vesting clauses of the United States Constitution , which vests the judicial power of the United States in federal courts, requires the supreme court, allows inferior courts, requires good behavior tenure for judges, and prohibits decreasing the salaries of judges. The judicial Power of the United States, shall be vested in one supreme Court, and in such inferior Courts as the Congress may from time to time ordain and establish. The Judges, both of the supreme and inferior Courts, shall hold their Offices during good Behaviour, and shall, at stated Times, receive for their Services a Compensation which shall not be diminished during their Continuance in Office. The Committee of Detail report reads slightly differently: [ 2 ] [ 3 ] The Judicial Power of the United States shall be vested in one supreme Court, and in such Inferior Courts as shall, when necessary, from time to time, be constituted by the Legislature of the United States. Clause 1: Vesting of judicial power and number of courts [ edit ] Main article: Judicial Vesting Clause Article III authorizes one Supreme Court, but does not set the number of justices that must be appointed to it. Article One, Section 3, Clause 6 refers to a "Chief Jus
perturbation.justification: The omission of the jurisdiction clause directly impacts the enforceability of the agreement in a specific court, potentially conflicting with international standards for choice of court agreements. The Law Explanation states that omitting the jurisdiction clause introduces ambiguity, creating potential jurisdictional disputes and undermines the agreement's enforceability. While the provided law snippets do not explicitly address choice of court agreements, the principle that the judicial power of the United States shall be vested in one Supreme Court, and in such inferior courts as the Congress may from time to time ordain and establish, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 is undermined. The omission of jurisdiction could lead to disputes about where legal proceedings should occur.

perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges and agrees that the Information is the confidential and/or propri etary and/or trade secret information of the other and the unauthorised use or disc losure of the Information could cause irreparable harm and significant injury to the other party for which that other party would have no adequate remedy at law. Therefore, each party shall have the right, in addition to any other rights it may have at law or in equity, to seek and obtain immediate injunctive relief in respect of any breach or potential breach of this Agreement by the other.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges and agrees that the Information is the confidential and/or propri etary and/or trade secret information of the other. Therefore, each party shall have the right, in addition to any other rights it may have at law or in equity, to seek and obtain injunctive relief in respect of any breach of this Agreement by the other.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'the unauthorised use or disclosure of the Information could cause irreparable harm and significant injury to the other party for which that other party would have no adequate remedy at law' weakens the argument for injunctive relief.  Courts often require proof of irreparable harm before granting an injunction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc) Regulations 2018 in the UK outlines remedies available for trade secret misappropriation, including injunctive relief (Regulation 6). The original text explicitly acknowledges the potential for irreparable harm, which is a key factor considered by courts when granting injunctions. Removing this acknowledgment makes it more difficult to obtain an injunction, potentially contradicting the intent of the regulations to provide effective remedies for trade secret violations, as the plaintiff has a higher burden of proving harm.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text includes an acknowledgement that a breach of the agreement could cause irreparable harm for which the other party would have no adequate remedy at law. The changed text removes this acknowledgement. The Law Explanation states that this explicit acknowledgement of potential irreparable harm is a key factor considered by courts when granting injunctions. Because the changed text removes language that would support a request for injunctive relief, it weakens the legal standing of that request. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation strongly indicates that a court is less likely to provide injunctive relief with the changed text than with the original text. This undermines the intent of remedies for trade secret violations and creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, both parties may provide certain confidential and proprietary information to each other in connection with the business purposes described in Exhibit A attach ed hereto ("the Business Purpose") and each desires that any such information provided shall be kept confidential by the other party; and WHEREAS, in consideration of the disclosure of such information each party is willing to keep the other party's information confidential in accordance with the terms and conditions set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, both parties may provide certain confidential and proprietary information to each other and each desires that any such information provided shall be kept confidential by the other party; and WHEREAS, in consideration of the disclosure of such information each party is willing to keep the other party's information confidential in accordance with the terms and conditions set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "in connection with the business purposes described in Exhibit A attach ed hereto ('the Business Purpose')" broadens the scope of confidential information covered by the NDA. Without limiting the confidential information to the specific 'Business Purpose' defined in Exhibit A, the agreement becomes overly broad and potentially unenforceable under UK law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Case law on Restraint of Trade (UK)
perturbation.law_explanation: UK case law on restraint of trade, such as Attwood v Lamont, holds that restrictive covenants, including confidentiality agreements, must be reasonable in scope to be enforceable. Removing the limitation to the defined 'Business Purpose' makes the confidentiality obligation overly broad, potentially constituting an unreasonable restraint of trade. This contradicts the principles established in case law, which requires such agreements to be narrowly tailored to protect legitimate business interests.

Accept and close Share this: Facebook Twitter Reddit LinkedIn WhatsApp Attwood v Lamont [1920] 3 KB 571 Doctrine of severability not applicable to covenant for protection of plaintiff’s entire business Facts The plaintiff was a tailor and general outfitter who employed the defendant as an assistant. In the contract of employment, the defendant agreed “either on his own account or on that of any wife of his or in partnership with or as assistant, servant, or agent to any other person, persons or company carry on or be in any way directly or indirectly concerned in any of the following trades or businesses; that is to say, the trade or business of a tailor, dressmaker, general draper, milliner, hatter, haberdasher, gentlemen’s, ladies’ or children’s outfitter at any place within a radius of ten miles of Kidderminster.” Issues The defendant established a business as a tailor outside the agreed radius but obtained orders within the radius. The Divisional Court held that the covenant was wider than what was reasonably necessary for the protection of the plaintiff’s business but it was severable and, accordingly, granted an injunction which referred only to the tailoring trade. Decision/Outcome The defendant appealed to the Court of Appeal which reversed the decision of the Divisional Court. The covenant was not severable because it was a single covenant for the protection of the plaintiff’s entire business and not several covenants for the protection of several businesses. In any case, even if the covenant could be
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the limitation of confidential information to the 'Business Purpose' defined in Exhibit A. The Law Explanation states that UK case law (Attwood v Lamont) requires restrictive covenants, like confidentiality agreements, to be reasonable in scope to be enforceable. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that removing the 'Business Purpose' limitation makes the confidentiality obligation overly broad. The scraped snippet of Attwood v Lamont supports this, indicating the court found a covenant wider than reasonably necessary for business protection to be problematic. Therefore, the changed text likely contradicts the principle of reasonable scope in confidentiality agreements.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that, for a period of 5 years from receipt of information from the other party hereunder, such party shall use the same means it uses to protect its ow n confidential and propriet ary information to prevent the disclosure and to protect the confidentiality of both: i) written information received from the other party which is marked or identified as "confidential"; and ii) oral or visual information identified as confidential at the time of disclosure which is reduced to written form and provided to the other in such written form promptly after such oral or visual disclosure, hereinafter collectively referre d to as "the Information".
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that, for a period of 5 years from receipt of information from the other party hereunder, such party shall prevent the disclosure and protect the confidentiality of both: i) written information received from the other party; and ii) oral or visual information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement that written information be marked "confidential" and that oral/visual information be reduced to written form and identified as confidential at the time of disclosure broadens the scope of protected information. This lack of clarity regarding what constitutes 'confidential information' creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability under UK law, as the counterparty doesn't have enough information as to what they are restricted from sharing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Attorney-General v Guardian Newspapers Ltd [1990] 1 AC 109
perturbation.law_citation: Attorney-General v Guardian Newspapers Ltd [1990] 1 AC 109
perturbation.law_explanation: The 'Spycatcher' case, Attorney-General v Guardian Newspapers Ltd [1990] 1 AC 109, illustrates the importance of clearly defining what constitutes confidential information. While this case centered around government secrets, the principle applies to commercial NDAs as well. By removing the requirement for marking written information as confidential and for reducing oral information to writing and identifying it as such, the modified agreement becomes overly broad and lacks the specificity necessary for enforceability. This contradicts the principles established in the Spycatcher case, which emphasizes the need for clarity and precision in defining the scope of confidentiality obligations.

The Observer Limited and others (Respondents) JUDGMENT Die Jovis 13° Octobris 1988 Upon Report from the Appellate Committee to whom was referred the Cause Her Majesty's Attorney General against The Observer Limited and others, That the Committee had heard Counsel on Tuesday the 14th, Wednesday the 15th, Thursday the 16th, Monday the 20th, Wednesday the 22nd and Thursday the 23rd days of June last, upon the Petition and Appeal of Her Her Majesty's Attorney General, of the Royal Courts of Justice, Strand, London, WC2, praying that the matter of the Order set forth in the Schedule thereto, namely an Order of Her Majesty's Court of Appeal of the 10th day of February 1988, as amended on the 14th and 31st days of March 1988, might be reviewed before Her Majesty the Queen in Her Court of Parliament and that the said Order might be reversed, varied or altered or that the Petitioner might have such other relief in the premises as to Her Majesty the Queen in Her Court of Parliament might seem meet; as upon the case of The Observer Limited, Donald Trelford, David Leigh, Paul Lashmar, Guardian Newspapers Limited, Peter Preston and Richard Norton Taylor, lodged in answer to the said appeal; and due consideration had this day of what was offered on either side in this Cause: It is Ordered and Adjudged, by the Lords Spiritual and Temporal in the Court of Parliament of Her Majesty the Queen assembled, That the said Order of Her Majesty's Court of Appeal of the 10th day of February 1988, as amended on the 14th and 31st days of March 1988, complained of in the said Appeal be, and the same is hereby, Affirmed and that the said Petition and Appeal be, and the same is hereby, dismissed this House: And it is further Ord
perturbation.justification: The original text provides specific conditions for information to be considered confidential (marked as confidential, reduced to written form). The changed text broadens the scope of what is considered confidential by removing these requirements. The Law Explanation references the 'Spycatcher' case, Attorney-General v Guardian Newspapers Ltd [1990] 1 AC 109, which highlights the importance of clearly defining confidential information. While the snippets provided do not directly contain the text of the 'Spycatcher' case, they do refer to it, indicating its relevance to confidentiality. By removing the specific requirements for identifying confidential information, the changed text introduces ambiguity and potentially makes the agreement overly broad and difficult to enforce. This contradicts the principle of clarity and precision in defining confidentiality obligations, as emphasized by the Law Explanation derived from the case.
